tweets.text:
Fertilizer prices are normalizing:

New Orleans granular urea prices, the most widely used nitrogen fertilizer, are down to $453.5 per short ton, the lowest since February 6th.

This marks a -36% decline from the mid-April peak, when prices surged to the highest since 2022 as the shutdown of the Strait of Hormuz effectively halted fertilizer shipments from the Middle East.

According to the Fertilizer Institute, countries directly affected by the Iran War account for almost half of all global urea exports.

The current decline has been driven by a sharp drop in seasonal demand, as US farmers have largely completed their spring fertilizer applications, leaving the domestic market oversupplied.

By comparison, Egypt's benchmark urea price has also plunged to ~$640.0 per short ton, the lowest since late February, though it remains significantly above US prices.

The fertilizer shock is easing.
tweets.full_text:
Fertilizer prices are normalizing:

New Orleans granular urea prices, the most widely used nitrogen fertilizer, are down to $453.5 per short ton, the lowest since February 6th.

This marks a -36% decline from the mid-April peak, when prices surged to the highest since 2022 as the shutdown of the Strait of Hormuz effectively halted fertilizer shipments from the Middle East.

According to the Fertilizer Institute, countries directly affected by the Iran War account for almost half of all global urea exports.

The current decline has been driven by a sharp drop in seasonal demand, as US farmers have largely completed their spring fertilizer applications, leaving the domestic market oversupplied.

By comparison, Egypt's benchmark urea price has also plunged to ~$640.0 per short ton, the lowest since late February, though it remains significantly above US prices.

The fertilizer shock is easing.

tweets.text:
The US job market is sending conflicting signals:

US hiring rate fell -0.3 percentage points in April, to 3.2%, the 2nd-lowest since the 2020 pandemic low and in-line with 2008 levels.

This metric has now been at or below 3.5% for 26 consecutive months.

Excluding the government sector, the private hiring rate declined -0.4 percentage points in April, to 3.5%, in-line with the 2020 low.

Both figures are also significantly below the 2001 recession levels.

Meanwhile, JOLTs and the May jobs report both crushed expectations.

Despite the bullish numbers, US hiring remains historically depressed.

tweets.text:
The US leveraged ETF market trading volume is exploding:

The total notional trading volume across US-listed leveraged and inverse ETFs surged to $90 billion on Tuesday, the highest on record.

This figure has more than TRIPLED over the last 12 months.

To put this into perspective, this represented ~50% of all assets under management across the entire leveraged and inverse ETF universe.

The 3x leveraged short semiconductor ETF, $SOXS, alone traded over 1.3 billion shares, the 3rd-largest single-session volume for any US-listed ETF over the last 20 years.

This is only behind the 2x leveraged long Nasdaq 100 ETF, $QLD, and the 2x leveraged long S&P 500 ETF, $SSO, both of which set their records during the 2008 Financial Crisis.

Risk appetite has never been higher.

BREAKING: Iran's President Pezeshkian responds to President Trump's threats to target Iranian infrastructure calling it "a sign of desperation."

Full statement:

"Critical infrastructures are the lifeblood of the people. Threats to target them, from transportation networks to the electricity and water industries, are not a show of strength but a sign of desperation in the face of a nation's will. Iran, relying on the knowledge and capabilities of its specialists, national unity, and solidarity, will stand firm against any pressure or threat," Pezeshkian says.

tweets.text:
🇨🇳 #China’s inflation numbers in May suggest that core inflation remains weak even as producer prices accelerated, suggesting that more work needs to be done to boost consumption, the Economic Daily reports, citing economists.

➡ The consumer price index rose 1.2% in May from a year earlier, bolstered by a 3.9% gain in industrial goods while food prices fell 1.7%, official data showed; CPI fell 0.1% from the previous month.

➡ The 1.1% year-on-year increase in core CPI, which excludes food and energy prices, slowed from April and remains relatively weak, giving room for further policy moves to stimulate consumption, the newspaper said, citing Feng Lin, executive research director at Golden Credit Rating.

➡ The producer price index’s 3.9% year-on-year gain continued to be higher than CPI inflation, suggesting benefits from higher factory prices are mainly concentrated in upstream resources and raw materials instead of consumption goods, the newspaper said, citing Pang Ming, a senior researcher with the National Institution for Finance & Development.

➡ Policymakers need to stabilize raw material prices to ease cost pressures on the middle and downstream businesses while boosting consumption, Pang was cited saying.

➡ CPI is expected to remain below 1.5% as food prices weigh on inflation, and fiscal policy has a bigger role to play, Yicai reported, citing Wen Bin, chief economist of China Minsheng Banking Corp.
*Link (Chinese): https://t.co/dnJ2aYaYKX

*Link (Chinese): https://t.co/dnJ2aYaYKX
tweets.full_text:
🇨🇳 #China’s inflation numbers in May suggest that core inflation remains weak even as producer prices accelerated, suggesting that more work needs to be done to boost consumption, the Economic Daily reports, citing economists.

➡ The consumer price index rose 1.2% in May from a year earlier, bolstered by a 3.9% gain in industrial goods while food prices fell 1.7%, official data showed; CPI fell 0.1% from the previous month.

➡ The 1.1% year-on-year increase in core CPI, which excludes food and energy prices, slowed from April and remains relatively weak, giving room for further policy moves to stimulate consumption, the newspaper said, citing Feng Lin, executive research director at Golden Credit Rating.

➡ The producer price index’s 3.9% year-on-year gain continued to be higher than CPI inflation, suggesting benefits from higher factory prices are mainly concentrated in upstream resources and raw materials instead of consumption goods, the newspaper said, citing Pang Ming, a senior researcher with the National Institution for Finance & Development.

➡ Policymakers need to stabilize raw material prices to ease cost pressures on the middle and downstream businesses while boosting consumption, Pang was cited saying.

➡ CPI is expected to remain below 1.5% as food prices weigh on inflation, and fiscal policy has a bigger role to play, Yicai reported, citing Wen Bin, chief economist of China Minsheng Banking Corp.
*Link (Chinese): https://t.co/dnJ2aYaYKX

*Link (Chinese): https://t.co/dnJ2aYaYKX

tweets.text: I got a lot of flack when I said this before, but I continue to think that model providers will end up being a lot like airlines: Critical for the global economy but highly commoditized, with huge capex requirements and low (and often negative) margins.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text: OpenAI is considering drastic price cuts as it seeks to win over customers from archrival Anthropic https://t.co/vwWcJJd1x8
tweets.full_text: I got a lot of flack when I said this before, but I continue to think that model providers will end up being a lot like airlines: Critical for the global economy but highly commoditized, with huge capex requirements and low (and often negative) margins.

tweets.text:
While I am not an AI expert, this new development may suggest that AI services may be evolving into an increasingly commoditized market, characterized by rising price sensitivity rather than durable moats and low demand elasticity. If this narrative continues to gain traction, it could mark a meaningful regime shift in how investors think about the AI revolution.

Once frontier AI companies begin competing aggressively on price, the industry’s revenue outlook could come under significant pressure—reminiscent of the destructive impact that food-delivery price wars had on China’s leading internet platforms.

At a time when global investors are heavily fixated on Anthropic’s rapid run-rate revenue growth, OpenAI’s apparent willingness to contemplate a price war could prove to be an unwelcome reality check—and potentially a punch bowl remover for an increasingly euphoric market.

I'd welcome any pushbacks or counterargument as to why this news may not be as bearish as I currently think.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text: * OpenAI Considers Drastic Price Cuts, Anticipating Costly War For Users With Anthropic - WSJ https://t.co/mbBXK1Th8n

thread_tweets.quoted_tweet_text: * OpenAI Considers Drastic Price Cuts, Anticipating Costly War For Users With Anthropic - WSJ https://t.co/mbBXK1Th8n
thread_tweets.full_text:
While I am not an AI expert, this new development may suggest that AI services may be evolving into an increasingly commoditized market, characterized by rising price sensitivity rather than durable moats and low demand elasticity. If this narrative continues to gain traction, it could mark a meaningful regime shift in how investors think about the AI revolution.

Once frontier AI companies begin competing aggressively on price, the industry’s revenue outlook could come under significant pressure—reminiscent of the destructive impact that food-delivery price wars had on China’s leading internet platforms.

At a time when global investors are heavily fixated on Anthropic’s rapid run-rate revenue growth, OpenAI’s apparent willingness to contemplate a price war could prove to be an unwelcome reality check—and potentially a punch bowl remover for an increasingly euphoric market.

I'd welcome any pushbacks or counterargument as to why this news may not be as bearish as I currently think.

thread_tweets.text: @aeybobby Cannot believe that I missed this post from Chamath. This is indeed the single biggest threat for this AI rally.

tweets.text:
Weekly RSI divergences are one of the most powerful long-term patterns I track. 
In September, I flagged a triple bearish divergence in Bitcoin's three-year rally - a pattern which put the bull market on a weak footing leading into Q4. 
https://t.co/qTS0DqqJvG
While I underestimated the bear market that followed (I assumed a 30% drawdown only), these weekly divergences have been prescient throughout my time: LTCM '98, the GFC in '08, and early 2020. Each time, the divergence flagged a trend losing momentum prior to a bear market.  
Fast forward to today, it's now the opposite signal. Last week's retest of the Feb low held a higher RSI. While the trend remains bearish on all timeframes, and another leg down is possible, the divergence says the inverse of September: selling momentum is decelerating. Similar setups occurred in 2015, 2019, and 2022. You will see the same patterns occur at the bottom of most equity bear markets. The pattern is not Bitcoin-specific. 
The Bear market may not be over but long-term allocators will be accumulating at these levels. Tactical investors will likely wait for the reversal first.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
Weekly BTC is flashing a triple bearish RSI divergence—momentum is bleeding. That’s why I keep talking about the next liquidity impulse from central banks.

I’ve seen this movie in equities: divergences flagged fragility into LTCM ’98, the GFC in ’08 (I was covering Asia https://t.co/RaC4We0KnH

tweets.text: Higher inflation is causing nominal and real retail sales to diverge. The good news is that gasoline prices have fallen about 40 cents from their highs, which should lead to a tamer CPI print in June. The bad news is that the cease-fire is really a “less-fire” and could easily become a “more-fire”.
